description: A signal materializes its value eagerly: once at creation, and again after every invalidating write, with no read required to trigger either. This is normative clauses 1 and 2 of *Signal eagerness* (reactive-graph.md), and it is the only thing that distinguishes an eager `Computed` from the lazy `Computed` it is built on -- reads of the two are indistinguishable by construction, which is exactly why `Signal` is a derived construct rather than a primitive. The observable is `computes_of`: the cumulative number of times a node's compute function has run. A lazy `Computed` and an eager `Computed` return identical VALUES for every read sequence in this fixture; they differ only in when compute ran, so a fixture asserting values alone cannot tell them apart and would pass against a binding whose eager construct returns a plain (never-materialized) `Computed`. The discriminating step is the `set_cell`, which carries no read: an eager signal shows 2 computes there, a lazy `Computed` still shows 1 and defers its recompute to the following read. Note that step 3 asserts the count is UNCHANGED by a read -- that catches the inverse defect, a binding that materialized eagerly and then recomputes again on access, which is a correctness-neutral but unbounded cost multiplier on every reader. Contract: #lzsignaleager clauses 1-2.

expect.note: clause 1: creation runs compute exactly once. A binding that defers materialization to first read shows 0 here and has an intermediate unset state a reader could observe.
the value was already materialized, so this read must NOT trigger a compute. A binding showing 2 here is recomputing on every access on top of its eager pull.

THE discriminating step, and deliberately carries no read. Clause 2: the value is fresh by the time the mutator returns. A lazy `Computed` shows 1 here -- it has been invalidated but not recomputed. This is the assertion a values-only fixture cannot make.
the eagerly materialized value is the correct one, and reading it still does not recompute
the == store-guard applies ahead of the puller: an equal write is not a write, so nothing invalidates and the puller does not run. A binding that pulls unconditionally on every set_cell shows 3.
